title: 3 Hidar — St. Zechariah the Prophet & The Assembly
saints: Sts. Cyriacus and Julietta (Quiriacus and Julitta), Martyrs
St. John of the Ladder (John Climacus), Monastic
narrative:
On the third day of Hidar, the monthly commemoration of the Prophet Zechariah arrives with his visions of the restored community — the measuring line stretched over Jerusalem, the four chariots patrolling the earth, the Branch growing from the remnant stump. In Hidar's apostolic atmosphere, Zechariah is the prophet of the community's structural renewal: his visions concern not only the interior life of individual souls but the visible, organized gathering of the people of God into a functioning Temple community. The apostolic vesting is not only the commissioning of individual missionaries; it is the construction and maintenance of the community in which the mission is rooted, and Zechariah is the prophet whose particular gift is to see how the community is to be rebuilt after it has been scattered. His vision of the young man measuring Jerusalem with a measuring line — who is told that the city will be inhabited like villages without walls, because God will be a wall of fire around it — is the theological charter of the apostolic community that does not depend on its own defenses.

Sts. Cyriacus and his mother Julietta are one of the most beloved parent-child martyrdom narratives in the tradition — Julietta a widow who was seized for her faith, and Cyriacus an infant of three years who, when the governor attempted to calm him by holding him, scratched the governor's face and declared himself a Christian. The infant martyr who confesses Christ before he can properly speak is the tradition's most extreme statement about the nature of baptismal grace: the faith given in the sacrament is not merely a cognitive adult commitment but an ontological reality that has transformed the very nature of the baptized, even the youngest. On the third day of the apostolic month, Cyriacus and Julietta present the community with the testimony that the apostolic vesting has no minimum age requirement — that the commission is given at the font, in the mystery, and may produce its witness at any moment of the baptized life.

St. John of the Ladder — John Climacus — was the seventh-century abbot of the monastery of Sinai whose work The Ladder of Divine Ascent is one of the most complete and most widely read manuals of the contemplative life in the entire Christian tradition. The Ladder describes thirty rungs of the spiritual life — corresponding to the thirty years of Christ's hidden life before His public ministry — ascending from the renunciation of the world through the battle with the passions, through the virtues of the active life, to the summit of the three theological virtues: faith, hope, and the love that surpasses all. In Hidar's apostolic context, John's Ladder is not a withdrawal from the community but the formation of the community's leaders: the monks who have climbed the Ladder become the ones best equipped to guide others, and the summit — love — is by definition turned outward.

Zechariah, Cyriacus and Julietta, and John Climacus together on the third day of Hidar present three dimensions of the apostolic vesting's community character. Zechariah provides the prophetic vision of the community's structural renewal. Cyriacus and Julietta provide the testimony that the community includes members at every stage of life and capacity, bound by the same sacramental reality. John Climacus provides the formation that produces the community's guides — the map of the interior life whose ultimate purpose is the love that turns outward to serve. The month of the Apostolic Vesting is three days old, and the shape of what it is forming is already visible.
